Multimeter Basics.clue: When measuring voltage across a component with a multimeter, this is how the meter is connected.
choices: In series with the component
In diagonal with the component
The meter does not need to be connected to the circuit
In parallel with the component
Multimeter Basics.explanation: Voltage is measured in parallel. Series connection (correct for current) would not give a valid voltage reading and could disturb the circuit.

Multimeter Basics.clue: Before measuring resistance with a multimeter, this must be ensured.
choices: The circuit is energized and running
The component is still hot from use
The component is installed in another circuit for reference
The circuit is de-energized and isolated from parallel paths
Multimeter Basics.explanation: Resistance measurement requires a de-energized circuit. Energized circuits disrupt the measurement and can damage the meter. Parallel paths must also be disconnected to avoid misleading readings.

Multimeter Basics.clue: A multimeter set to the ohms range shows 'OL.' This is what the reading means.
choices: The meter is overloaded and damaged
The resistance exceeds the meter's selected
The circuit is hot with voltage
The meter is out of calibration
Multimeter Basics.explanation: 'OL' means 'over-limit' — the resistance is greater than the meter's scale can show. In troubleshooting, this almost always means an open circuit. The meter itself is fine, the circuit is open.

Multimeter Basics.clue: This multimeter test is the MOST effective way to confirm a suspected broken wire.
choices: AC voltage test
Continuity (or ohms) test with the circuit
Oscilloscope waveform analysis
Visual inspection only, with no meter
Multimeter Basics.explanation: Continuity/resistance testing directly confirms whether the wire is connected. AC voltage tests are for energized circuits. Oscilloscopes are overkill for a simple continuity question. Visual inspection alone cannot identify internal conductor breaks with intact insulation.

Multimeter Basics.clue: This is the primary function of a multimeter.
choices: To measure multiple electrical
To generate electrical signals
To convert AC to DC
To amplify weak signals
Multimeter Basics.explanation: A multimeter measures — it does not generate signals, convert power, or amplify. The three core measurements are voltage, current, and resistance.

Multimeter Advanced.clue: While testing a 24 V DC circuit, this could cause a multimeter to display 235 V.
choices: The circuit has somehow multiplied its own supply voltage tenfold
The aircraft battery has gained voltage while sitting on the shelf
Wrong range, wrong AC/DC mode, damaged probes, or induced ghost reading
The meter is working correctly and the high reading is accurate as shown
Multimeter Advanced.explanation: A 235 V reading on a 24 V DC circuit is almost always a meter-setup or ghost-voltage issue. The circuit itself cannot spontaneously produce 10× its source voltage.

Multimeter Advanced.clue: If you measure 0 V across a component in an active circuit, this is a possible cause.
choices: The circuit must be powered off
The meter must be broken
The component is shorted internally
The component is functioning normally
Multimeter Advanced.explanation: 0 V with current flowing means zero resistance (shorted). 0 V with no current flowing can occur when an upstream break eliminates the current, leaving no voltage drop to measure. Either way, it merits investigation.

Multimeter Advanced.clue: This is what a megohmmeter (megger) is used to test.
choices: Total physical wire length
Signal frequency in hertz
Insulation resistance conductor-to-ground
Ground bonding in milliohms only
Multimeter Advanced.explanation: Meggers test insulation resistance at high voltage. Wire length is measured by TDR. Signal frequency is measured by a frequency counter or oscilloscope. Ground bonding is measured by a milliohmmeter, not a megger.

Multimeter Advanced.clue: This is an important safety precaution when using a megohmmeter (megger).
choices: Leave all avionics connected for a realistic test
Apply continuous voltage with no cool-down
Disconnect the circuit from its power source AND from any
Ignore manufacturer maximum test voltages
Multimeter Advanced.explanation: Megger voltage damages solid-state electronics and avionics. Isolate before testing. Post-test discharge is important to handle safely. Manufacturer voltage limits must be respected.

Multimeter Advanced.clue: This is the primary use of a milliohmmeter.
choices: Measuring very low resistances in the milliohm range
Measuring high-voltage insulation resistance
Generating test signals
Measuring signal frequency
Multimeter Advanced.explanation: Milliohmmeters measure very low resistances (milliohms). Insulation resistance is for meggers. Signal generation requires a function generator. Frequency measurement uses a counter or scope.

Other Test Equipment.clue: An oscilloscope is used to visualize this.
choices: Electrical resistance
Magnetic field strength
Pressure
Electrical signal waveforms
Other Test Equipment.explanation: An oscilloscope displays waveforms — voltage vs time. Resistance is measured by ohmmeters; magnetic fields by gaussmeters; pressure by pressure gauges or ADCs.

Other Test Equipment.clue: This is the primary purpose of a center punch in an avionics repair shop.
choices: To drive nails into hardwood
To test electrical continuity
To remove burrs from drilled holes
To make an indent that guides a drill
Other Test Equipment.explanation: A center punch creates a small pilot indentation that guides a drill bit, keeping it from wandering. Nail driving uses a hammer. Continuity testing uses a meter. Burr removal uses a deburring tool or countersink.

Other Test Equipment.clue: When using a manual screwdriver, this is the MOST important factor for preventing damage to the screw head.
choices: Using the longest available screwdriver
Matching the driver tip exactly to the screw head type
Using maximum torque regardless of the screw
Lubricating the driver tip with grease
Other Test Equipment.explanation: Correct tip-to-screw match and full engagement distribute torque evenly. Longest driver is not a factor. Max torque is dangerous. Greasing the driver tip would just make it slip.

Other Test Equipment.clue: This is the function of a wire crimper.
choices: To cut wire to length
To compress a terminal or connector
To strip insulation from a wire
To apply solder to a joint
Other Test Equipment.explanation: A crimper compresses a terminal onto a wire. Cutting is done by cutters. Stripping is done by a stripper. Soldering uses a soldering iron.

Other Test Equipment.clue: This is why a technician performs a 'wiggle test' near connectors before finalizing an installation.
choices: To identify intermittent connections
To add torque to the fastener
To increase the signal voltage
To test the software load
Other Test Equipment.explanation: The wiggle test reveals intermittent physical connections. It is a diagnostic technique, not a torque application, voltage test, or software check.

Extended Coverage.clue: During a voltage test on a 24 V DC circuit, this could cause a multimeter to display 235 V.
choices: The 24 V source has suddenly increased its output roughly tenfold
Wrong meter mode (AC vs DC), wrong range, damaged probe, or ghost voltage
The internal resistance of the component has gone effectively infinite
The meter is operating on a perfectly functioning low battery
Extended Coverage.explanation: A 10x reading on a fixed DC source is a measurement-setup artifact: wrong mode, wrong range, damaged lead, or induced ghost voltage on a floating point. The source cannot produce 235 V from a 24 V bus, and component resistance does not raise applied voltage.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: Scenario: You are conducting a voltage test on a 24V DC circuit, and the multimeter shows a reading of 235V What could be the cause of this small voltage drop?

Extended Coverage.clue: This technique BEST confirms that a specific switch is causing a parasitic battery drain.
choices: Swap the suspect switch with a known-good one and wait a full week to compare
Check the switch housing for visible cracks and surface damage under lighting
Install an in-line ammeter and pull breakers one at a time watching the drain
Run a megger insulation test on the switch contacts at several voltage ranges
Extended Coverage.explanation: The breaker-pull method with an in-line ammeter isolates the drain to a specific branch. Swap testing is slow. Visual inspection misses internal faults. Megger testing is for insulation, not parasitic leakage.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: If a technician suspects a faulty switch is causing a parasitic draw, how can they BEST confirm this?

Extended Coverage.clue: This is the diagnostic approach a technician uses to verify a suspected switch is causing parasitic draw.
choices: Measuring the switch body with an infrared thermometer
Measure battery current and pull breakers until the draw drops
Checking the switch against its part number in the illustrated parts catalog
Listening for a faint buzz at the switch with a stethoscope
Extended Coverage.explanation: A current measurement on the battery lead plus systematic breaker pulling — one at a time until the draw returns to normal — is the verification technique. Temperature, parts-catalog lookup, and audio methods cannot positively identify a milliampere-range drain.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: Scenario: You are conducting a voltage test on a 24V DC circuit, and the multimeter shows a reading of 235V. What could be the cause of this small voltage drop?

Extended Coverage.clue: To ensure accurate readings with a micrometer, a technician should do this.
choices: Zero the mic, use the ratchet for consistent force, clean faces, seat squarely
Tighten the thimble firmly by hand to guarantee solid anvil contact
Measure each part only a single time, to keep readings consistent
Use body heat to warm the part before taking the measurement
Extended Coverage.explanation: Accurate micrometer technique relies on zero verification before use, consistent contact force through the ratchet or friction thimble, clean measuring faces, and square seating. Hand overtightening distorts readings, single readings miss error, and warming the part causes thermal expansion.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: When using a micrometer, what should be done to ensure accurate measurements?

Extended Coverage.clue: This is the principal application of a milliohmmeter in avionics work.
choices: Stressing cable insulation at high DC test voltage
Very low resistance — bonding straps, relay contacts
Viewing signal waveform shape versus time on screen
Counting digital pulses per second on a databus line
Extended Coverage.explanation: A milliohmmeter reads very low resistance with a four-wire Kelvin connection — bonding straps, relay contacts, and similar low-resistance joints. Insulation stress testing is the megger's role. Waveform viewing is the oscilloscope. Pulse counting uses a frequency counter.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: A milliohmmeter is primarily used to:

final.clue: When using a Time Domain Reflectometer (TDR) to shoot a coaxial cable fault, an upward (positive) reflection spike on the trace indicates what type of cable fault?
choices: A direct short to ground
An open circuit or break
Water intrusion
Normal impedance matching
final.explanation: A TDR trace reflects upward for a high-impedance fault (an open/break) and downward for a low-impedance fault (a short).

final.clue: On an oscilloscope capturing an ARINC 429 signal, you observe a square wave that fails to cross the 0V baseline, instead fluctuating between +10V and +5V. What does this indicate?
choices: Normal bipolar return-to-zero operation
A loss of the HI or LO wire, preventing
The baud rate is too fast
The termination resistor is missing
final.explanation: ARINC 429 uses a differential bipolar signal (+10V, 0V, -10V). If the scope sees it entirely floating positive, it means one of the legs (A or B) is broken or improperly referenced to ground.

final.clue: When testing a high-voltage generator feeder cable with a Megohmmeter (Megger), why must you discharge the cable immediately after the test?
choices: To reset the meter
To clear the error code
To dissipate the high voltage capacitance stored in the cable
To prevent a false short reading on the next test
final.explanation: A Megger injects high voltage (e.g., 500V or 1000V). A long run of insulated wire acts as a capacitor and can store a dangerous charge that will shock the technician if not discharged to ground.
